title: 2026/05/05 [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2026-05-05 - Justice Committee - Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill [Part 1]
description: Tuesday, 5 May 2026 | Justice Committee Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Te Mana Raraunga Lara Greaves Larissa Renfrew Logan Hamley Community Housing Aotearoa Paul Gilberd, Chief Executive Chris Glaudel, Deputy Chief Executive Hui E! Community Aotearoa Dr Katie Bruce, Tangata Tiriti CE Patrick Gemmell, Tangata Whenua CE Helen Viggers (on behalf of Peter Crampton) Leonard Cook Te Wahine Ora o Hakepa Foundation Hakepa, Spokesperson Data Iwi Leaders Group Rahui Papa, Chair Kirikowhai Mikaere, Lead Technician Retail NZ Carolyn Young, CEO Denise Garland, Retail NZ Advocacy Manager Indigenous Data Authority Lisa Pizzoni, Founder Jonathan Godfrey Tze Ming Mok Stephen Haslett Kay Saville-Smith New Zealand Council of Trade Unions Te Kauae Kaimahi Sandra Grey, President Jack Foster, Policy Analyst New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Bryce Lyall, Member of the Law Society’s Public Law Committee New Zealand College of Public Health Medicine Dr Vanessa Selak, Associate Professor Professor Sir Collin Tukuitonga, President Hauora Taiwhenua Rural Health Network Dr Grant Davidson, CEO Te Ngakau Kahukura Moira Clunie MNZM, Co-Lead Koi Tu Centre for Informed Futures Hema Sridhar, Deputy Director
descriptionSummary: Data and Statistics (Census) Amendment Bill. Te Mana Raraunga. Lara Greaves

chunks.text: Right. Who's our first submission? I am. In person. It needs to be in person. Yes. We have Te Mana o te Data Māori Data Trust. Not here. Okay. Let's go to the Zoom. Do we know if the Zoom's ready? Yeah. Are they online? Yes. So I'll just...
And no special start. We'll leave now, they might come back.

Good morning. Kia ora. Morena. We've got 10 minutes with you. Thank you for being available a little bit early. But looking forward to hearing what you've got to say. So we've got 10 minutes, as I say. So please leave some time for some questions if you can. Thank you. Thank you. Sorry, we just got the thing that our internet connection is unstable, so let us know. Classic.

Classic for a select committee, so yeah. Thank you. We'll take it away. Te Mana Roranga, the Māori Data Sovereignty Network, brings together more than 800 Māori researchers, practitioners, and entrepreneurs who are passionate about data and Māori rights. As a group focused predominantly on advocacy, Te Mana Roranga is different to Te Kāhui Roranga Charitable Trust, who are party to the Mana Oraite Agreement.

However, we support the submission on this bill made by the Data Iwi Leaders Group. Te Mana Roranga also engages with the Global Indigenous Data Alliance, supporting the realization of global indigenous data rights through the care principles and supporting UNDRIP and other international and national treaties and conventions. Today, representing Te Mana Roranga are three members of Te Whakapū, the steering group. Dr Lara Greaves, an associate professor in politics at Victoria University of Wellington with expertise in Māori politics, survey

development, quantitative methods in Māori and LGBTQ+ demography. Dr Logan Hamley, a senior researcher at Whakawai Research Services, a Ngāti Hauāz lead research center. And Larissa Renfrew, a PhD student at the University of Auckland who has conducted research related to Māori data sovereignty and ethics, Māori identity, youth wellbeing, and demography.

Collectively, our group has also used the RDI in research, and also been consulted by Stats NZ about the approach to LGBTQ+ topics in the census. TMR's position on this bill is shaped by our understanding of Māori data as both a strategic asset and a living taonga to Māori. This includes both data produced by the census or the new proposed approach. Our position is also formed by the principles of Māori data sovereignty as foundational to Te Mana Roranga's advocacy work.

Te Mana Roranga welcomes the opportunity to submit on this bill, and we want to begin by acknowledging that there are genuine positives in the proposed model. More frequent data releases will be valuable. The flexibility to tailor surveys for different subpopulations is promising, and we commend the focus on meeting the needs of communities, including Iwi Māori. But we have serious concerns, and we believe this legislation is premature. The move to administrative census removes the enumerated census before we know the replacement works.

Experts across the board have raised grave concerns about data quality, and Statistics New Zealand's own independent review has indicated that high-quality statistics from administrative data will not be achievable by 2028. For Māori, the stakes are particularly high. Administrative data is deficit-framed. It is collected at the most vulnerable moments in people's lives and does not tell the full story of our communities.

Critical variables like Māori descent, Iwi affiliation, and te reo Māori fluency have no clear plan still. And Māori descent is not just a data question. It underpins our electoral system and our constitutional arrangements. There is also a deeper issue of data sovereignty and consent. This bill expands and links administrative data without free, prior, and informed consent, and without adequate Māori governance, and without upholding the Mana Oraite Agreement.

Aotearoa has the opportunity to build a world-leading trustworthy data system, and this bill, as it stands, moves us in the wrong direction. Yeah. And as for our recommendations, we kind of considered, okay, so there's been this process, it's been quite a long consultation process, a methodological process within Stats NZ, and we can definitely see some flaws in that process and really want to know what is the best model going forward. How can we make this work considering costs, considering the various issues we've discussed today, and that various other

speakers will discuss today? We think that an independent review of the methods, of the governance, of the decisions made so far is really our recommendation, and making sure that there are-... reviewers with expertise in Māori data sovereignty, Māori data governance. We see a real missed opportunity here to create our own model, our own data ecosystem.

Not just copying a Nordic model, but making something unique to Aotearoa, New Zealand that brings in Te Kahui Turoa's Māori data governance model, which has some great features for everyone, but would also uphold Tititi and do great in terms of building a trustworthy data system, a robust data system, and one that just works better for everyone and ultimately saves a lot of money. We think it's great that the bill moves towards greater engagement than just every five years with Māori, but the way that administrative data works is that decisions are made within agencies

and made within Stats NZ that need greater review and greater engagement with Māori. Because we know for population, someone will make a decision that just seems like a little technical tweak here and there, but it can have a lot of effect on someone's day-to-day life or how they interact with the state and the system.

So ultimately, we really think that this is a missed opportunity to create a great data system that governs our data as New Zealanders and as Māori, as taonga, upholds Māori data sovereignty, governs data as taonga, but also as a national strategic asset. So ultimately, we side with a lot of the experts that are saying there are great methodological issues, but also point to the suite of governance issues as well with this move away from the enumerated census. Kia ora. Thank you for listening.

Good. Thank you very much. Now, I've got a couple of questions, or three actually. Camilla Belich first. Kia ora. Thank you very much for your oral submission and your written submission. I note the strong points you've made around Māori data sovereignty, Tititi, and also recommend an independent review. But I just wanted to ask in terms of if this bill was to go ahead as it's currently outlined, acknowledging those strong points of your submission, what, if any, issues do you see around the actual administrative data and how that would

look in terms of its falseness? Do you have any concerns around that type of result that might come out of the use of administrative data? Absolutely. I think it was really well laid out by the independent review that was done by Stats NZ that indicated that we just won't have that high-quality administrative data by 2028.

So if this does roll out, there are going to be major gaps, and those gaps are going to cause a lot of harm for Māori. Mm. And Māori descent. Yeah. That's something that our electoral system is based on, is it's one of the lowest quality indicator with the lowest rate of coverage. Mm-hmm. And for us as analysts, as scientists, the first thing you do with a new method is you make sure it is super robust, and there's always going to be naysayers, but you want it to be pretty good that there's- Mm... critiques around the edges, but people think it's robust before you bring

something else in. And for Māori descent in particular, that's not the case. Mm. All right. I've got a question from Mr. Todd Stevenson. Mine's kind of a supplementary, I think, to Camilla's, because I was looking at paragraph 36 of your submission, where you talk about some of you have obviously used the IDI, and you were disappointed with the quality and breadth of the data. You got a couple of examples of what you'd mean by that?

So basically, when you go to the IDI, you think, okay, you're going to have millions and millions of cases of potential-- Normally I do surveys, so it's like, wow, N equals four million. Excellent. The problem you then have is that these data were collected when people are, say, getting arrested, or they're collected when someone's in hospital.

So there's all sorts of very strange kind of patterns in the data. Mm-hmm. Like missing data where you wouldn't expect it, places where people's ethnicity changes- Mm... just over data sets at any given snapshot in time. So there's a lot of issues. And I would say at the moment, my bedtime reading is Walgreen and Walgreen, "Register-Based Statistics", and I would recommend it to the select committee.

It's an excellent book that Stats NZ have said they based this approach on. The Nordic model, they patched all of that and made sure that there weren't issues across data sets before they implemented an administrative census. So that's what you would say is that you need to make sure that you don't have these gaps or this weird inconsistency in the data, and that you have a very clear, transparent, robust approach to where there are inconsistencies, especially for things like the Māori Descent Indicator.

Mm. Thanks. All right. Hey, thank you very much for your submission. It's been very helpful and very- Thank you... comprehensive, your submission. So thank you for this morning. Right. Thanks, everyone. Now, we're going into the next one, Community Housing Aotearoa. Are they available?
They're online, Chair. Okay.

Good morning. Nice to see you. Are you on your own this morning? I believe Paul will be joining me, but I-... will go ahead and kick off. Morena, ko toko. Chris Gladdell, toko inuma. I'm the Deputy CE with Community Housing Aotearoa, and thank you for the opportunity to address you this morning.

Won't speak to the points in our submission. We share concerns about the changes that are taking place, and specifically, I'd like to give a couple of examples from current work that we have underway. Our work relies on accurate data to understand what's going on with households and housing in Aotearoa, New Zealand, and currently doing a bit of work for a community trust in the South Island.

And the ability to look at, say, rental housing stress, changes in rates of home ownership, of overcrowding, rely on data out of the census currently. And with the change in approach, what we understand from engagement with Stats New Zealand is that for communities under 5,000 in population, they're just going to become invisibleYou really won't be able to get down into the details like we currently are in being able to separate households into income bands, into renters and homeowners, and then understanding their levels of housing stress and other outcomes.

And we find it somewhat interesting at the time that we're moving toward and progressing with the social investment approach that we support, and relying on the IDI to be able to understand outcomes from interventions. And with these changes, we have serious concerns about the ability to understand, especially in smaller communities, and as was noted by the prior respondents, say, by ethnicity, and with different Iwi affiliation.

And so we think that really putting in place the fundamentals of the administrative data approach needs to be done before we move away from the enumerated census because of that inability to really look down into communities, say, like districts in Waimate or McKenzie that have a few thousand households there.

Now, going forward, we've made a number of recommendations on how we think we can mitigate some of the worst impacts, but we really see the opportunity to do a pause and to put in place the safeguards that are needed to ensure that we have the ability to have accurate data on which to make informed decisions, which we believe will be lost.

I'm currently also participating on a Stats NZ user group around the housing domains in trying to understand how we can identify different data sets to replace what's going to be lost. And some things are fairly fundamental. And in that work, we know that, say, renters about reliance on the MB bond data is only providing about 85% coverage.

So we're losing 15% of renters, which we know is a growing portion of all households. We also know that there are problems in matching LINZ title data to addresses within Stats NZ, which is important to understand coverage of, say, ownership of trusts and owner occupation.

And so there are some known deficiencies in the proposed approach that's going to fundamentally change the ability to have good data for housing. I think we all recognize the fundamental importance of a home and how important it is to have a stable place to raise a family, to grow connections with community.

And we would urge the committee to look at the timing of these changes and what needs to be put into place to ensure that we can continue to have robust data on which to make informed decisions that are also fundamental to the resource management reforms and projections that are going to be required to plan for growth over 30 years. And if we don't have a good understanding of the demographic and other changes within our population in areas, how can we adequately plan for that future growth and ensure that we're able to deliver

outcomes that are better than what we're getting today. So with that, I'll hand it across to Paul, who's joined us. Morning, Paul. My apologies for lateness. Andrew, you must be running ahead of schedule. We are. Excellent. Well, thank you for the time you've given to us.

I'm going to speak more to the logic of this proposed change and suggest whether it's in a wellbeing context or in a social investment context. I'm going to suggest the cost of not doing the census is a quantum far greater than the cost of doing it.

And I'm questioning the logic for this change. Because in a world, let's call it a social investment world, which is where we are politically at this point in time, the costs of not identifying groups and cohorts, as Chris has just spoken to, who are our most vulnerable, the risk is that we don't identify and intervene upstream in terms of the causes of those issues.

So let's talk briefly about homelessness, which is one example that's in the public discourse at present. It is far more expensive to treat people in an emergency department or to have them in a prison than it is to address the upstream root causes to address that. And everyone we've spoken to including one of the most eminent statisticians in the world, Liam Cook, who was our chief statistician and was also the chief statistician in the UK for a number of years, has told us the dangers of not doing a census and the

risks of not identifying those people inside households, because this is the one opportunity we have as a nation to look inside a household in disaggregated forms into gender, into ethnicity, into age. And we have, as you know, a huge wave of older adults coming through to low fixed income retirement with no assets and no provisions for their adequate housing. And so I'm suggesting to you that the logic of getting rid of the census is fundamentally flawed, even if you're just looking at it through a financial lens.

And people internationally who have suggested this in other jurisdictions have lost their jobs over this. So it's something we're taking very seriously. And so I'd ask you to consider that in your deliberations. And alsoNot to put too fine a point on it, but making homelessness illegal and then not counting them as homeless people does not fix the problem.

So where's the logic for this change? All right. Hey, thank you for those comments. Can I ask you, you've made some helpful recommendations towards the end of your submission, one of them which is increasing the sample size from 50,000 to 100,000, which our professor talked about last week.

My understanding of the approach is obviously sample size is pretty important. You've said populations under 5,000 basically get missed out. By increasing it to 100,000 and then your second limb was about better community work in terms of identifying those sort of very specific communities that should be subject to some form of census. How far does that go towards addressing the problem?

It improves the data quality faster. So I mentioned in my opening remarks that it was until 2032 that we anticipated being able to get down to communities of about 5,000 in population. And so by doubling it would bring that forward and give you greater insights faster.

Right. Okay. Yeah. Carry on. So it still doesn't replace fundamentally. You'll still have gaps, but it does improve. If this is the approach that's chosen to go, it will help to mitigate the worst of it and bring data forward more timely. And I'm not trying to be contentious, but just you talked about homelessness.

Even in the census, we all realize that a census, even now, the last census had quite a significant element that wasn't counted, couldn't be counted, and homelessness is notoriously difficult. So even if you carry the existing census, wouldn't we still be having trouble trying to get a better understanding of homelessness, to use your example?

Definitely. I think significant improvements have been made in process there internationally. So snowballing is a well-known and accepted methodology for that in terms of getting out. What we know is that homeless communities all over New Zealand, even in small town and large centers, have a community amongst themselves. And so with some diligent work using that technique, it can be a very effective method because they know how to find each other, to put it bluntly.

And I think even in communities below the 5,000 threshold, and it's worth mentioning, for example, the housing crisis that we have right now on the Chatham Islands and also in the Rakiura, Stewart Island. Sure, it's a population of 450 people, but we have every summer an older adult housing crisis there which would be completely blind to if we were using government-sourced data because no one from Stewart Island who's a lifelong Islander is going to get on a boat, go to Invercargill to an MSD office to ask to go on a list for a house which does not

exist in their community. So we're really worried about the rural damage that could be done by removing the census. Okay. Hey, thank you for your time. Very good submissions, actually. We've got good submissions on this bill, but yours is a very good one. Thank you very much. Thank you. Cheers. Right now we're going to our next submitter, hopefully Dr. Katie Bates.
Bruce, sorry, Dr. Katie Bruce or Patrick Gemmell. Are you- Patrick's on the phone. Patrick.

Are they there on the screen? Here we are. Patrick or Katie, are you available? Good morning, Patrick. How are you going? And Katie, Dr. Katie Boxall, I think. Thank you. We've got 10 minutes with you. Just please leave a little bit of time for questions. Floor is yours.

Will do. Kia ora tatou. Hui e. We are the national peak body connecting Tangata Whenua and community in the voluntary sector. Hui e supports the modernized census if it means more equitable, more frequent, and more community-centered data. Tangata Whenua and community and the voluntary sector contribute immeasurably to the wellbeing of Aotearoa.

So the census must be designed, in our view, to see us. We welcome your questions, and we urge the committee to strengthen the bill so the census becomes more equitable, more community-centered, and not less. Administrative data first risks deepening inequity. Our data mapping. Existing data, government data already is, in our view, piecemeal, small scale, and disjointed, particularly across Statistics New Zealand, the Ministry of Social Development, Oranga Tamariki, Department of Internal Affairs, and the Ministry of Business

Innovation and Employment. Admin data reflects who government already counts in our view. Marginalized groups, voluntary networks, informal groups, rural organizations are least likely to appear reliably. Annual outputs must include community indicators.

Annual publication from 2030 is welcome, but without explicit provision, will default to populations and, in our view, housing only. This must embed an annual framework, voluntary rates, community participation, and social cohesion.And please do not leave this to discretion. Katie?

Oh, kia ora, Patrick. Tēnā koutou. It's a real pleasure to talk to you about this really important issue today. We have been trying as a peak body across the sector to understand the data that's happening across 27,000 charities, over 100,000 community organizations, and we have real concerns that if we are not able to do this with what's currently available, then what is now on offer in this bill will just make that harder. Because essentially, decision-makers inside of the sector, but also decision-makers in government, cannot make good funding decisions,

cannot make good policy decisions unless you have the right evidence. And of course, counting just what you already know inside of government just doesn't give you that broader picture. The sector essentially is largely invisible in the way that we present budget, and in the way that current administrative data is collected. And I can give you a tiny example of having done a review into policing, communities and policing outside of this mahi, that police don't collect data on disabled people that they interact with.

And so of course, there's just a complete void and a complete vacuum of information there, even though we know that that overlap is really significant. Now, the Nonprofit Institution Satellite Account is one of those examples of something that gave us such great in-depth information, and we're reliant on that now from 2018. So pre-COVID times, we're needing to make decisions about our sector, about its resilience, about its future from something that is so outdated.

And so what we think is that we need to essentially, given our sector is under acute pressure, really concentrate this modernization on the indicators that are going to make the most difference and be useful for decision making. One of those things, for example, is that Māori data sovereignty must be a design principle.

It's not a group to consult with outside, but it's actually needing to be designed in right from the beginning about what data is being collected, how it's being collected, how it's being shared, and then how it informs decision making all the way through, and this bill in its current form treats Māori data interests as a consultation item and not foundational design principle, and the bill should require co-design with Māori.

And we recommend reinstating Te Kupenga which gave such a holistic and real insight into community-level data. So we hope that you can see the value outside of government decision making, and of course to inform decision making of community indicators and community regular and robust data to inform our sector and to inform government making decisions. Kia ora. Welcome any questions.

Okay, thank you. I've got Todd Stevenson. Oh, thanks. And just Katie, to pick up one of your points, can you explain what the Nonprofit Institution Satellite Accounts were, how they operated and what kind of data that gave you access to? And were they part of the census? Because-- Yeah. So run by Statistics New Zealand, and I think probably one of your next submitters, Len Cook, will be able to give you a really more in-depth answer than I can. But what they essentially gave us is they give a definition of the sector.

They look at all the different components. So, all the different sub-sectors, the size and scale and economic contribution of those different parts of our sector. So you look at social services, housing, child and youth services, all of those different parts, and the environment, arts, culture, heritage. And what they do is ask those organizations critical questions about the conditions for the sector, and that is the only real in-depth information that we have.

And was that part of the main five-year census, or was that a separate piece of work, Katie? So my understanding is that it's a separate piece of work that is done by Statistics New Zealand. Okay. Now I've got Camilla Village. Kia ora, thank you for your submission. And thanks for highlighting the Te Kupenga and the Nonprofit Satellite report as well.

In terms of your submission, some of the other submitters to this bill have said that they're kind of opposed to the change from a move to administrative data, but your submission seems to be saying, let's increase the amount of administrative data or increase the number of data sets that we have, and make sure of course that they have data sovereignty and good principles in them.

Is that a correct reading of your submission, that you're not opposed to the change to administrative data, but you just want the data sets that are relied on to be increased and improved? Not quite. So we are not opposed to the modernization of the census and the way in which we've got administrative data and we've got the census, so let's look at them together and really understand what are the limitations of administrative data, and if we rely on that alone, we're missing out hugely, as you heard from the last submission, and those gaps, particularly those

marginalized communities.But we do have that administrative data, so let's use it. Let's not pretend we don't have it, but we need to make sure it's all linked up as part of a bigger understanding across the sector. But the census just gives us so much more than they ever can.

Okay. Hey, thank you for your submission this morning. Very enlightening for that. And great, hope you have a good morning. Right now, we've got Mr. Peter Crampton by Zoom as well, haven't we? Yes. It's Helen on- Helen? Oh, that's in a different sheet. Okay.

She's appearing- Helen on behalf of Peter Crampton. Right. Oh, yeah. Here, I better use this sheet then. Right. Helen, are you with us? Yes, I hope you can hear me. I'm trying to make you see me. The- We can't see you, but we can hear you. That's the main thing. Okay. Apologies on not being able to be seen. My video is not turning on.

No, that's all right. That's all right, Helen. Okay. So I'm Dr. Helen Figgers in place of Professor Peter Crampton, and presenting on behalf of the team that has created the New Zealand Deprivation Index for every census from nineteen ninety-one to twenty twenty-three. We used data from the full enumeration census of population and dwellings to quantify the relative socioeconomic deprivation of small areas, generally only one to two hundred people, often SA1s, across New Zealand. NZDep was initially created to help with resource allocation in the health sector, but

has also found a wide range of use in other sectors, like ECA and community law centers. NZDep is useful precisely because it has an intensely place-based measure that identifies where people, family, and households experience relative socioeconomic deprivation. The full enumeration census of population and dwellings has also been intensely place-based, tying the characteristics of dwellings, households, and people to the areas in which they live.

There are two main replacements to the full enumeration census, the Census Attribute Survey and the Admin Population Census. So firstly, the proposed Census Attribute Survey is place-based, but it won't count everyone, and it will average results over much larger areas than NZDep uses.

Only those with five thousand people or more. This means no information available at the SA1 level and no information available for almost all SA2s, and only for about half of SA3s. So it's not going to be useful for the creation of future NZDeps. Pockets of socioeconomic deprivation, sometimes only one or two blocks long, can exist inside well-off areas like Karori, and similarly, well-off pockets can exist inside poorer locations.

And capturing this small area diversity is one of the really important uses of NZDep. The other thing, the Administrative Population Census. This will aim to capture everyone, but admin data is collected at the person level and is not intrinsically place-based. Connections to place have to be worked out, and as recently as March this year, Stats New Zealand reported an address consistency of only eighty-seven percent. Similarly, although Stats New Zealand is investigating how to calculate families and households from

person data, it cannot yet adequately do so. Also in March, it reported a calculated household consistency of only sixty-eight percent. Families and households are vital subgroups of society, and people's experience in them is part of their socioeconomic experience. And so they're important in an NZDep.

There are also quality issues with admin data. The information will only be as timely as the person's last interaction with government services. This means for some people, it's going to be wildly out of date or nonexistent, and for others, extremely current. Whether or not it's out of date could also be socioeconomically patterned, and the accuracy of data will be affected by people's willingness to share information with the different services and the computer systems and programming within which it's stored.

Overall, of the nine variables we used to calculate NZDep twenty twenty-three, only three were at the person level and are already confirmed to be available in some way inside the Administrative Population Census. The other six at the family, household, and dwelling levels may or may not become available. Some probably will, others almost certainly won't. We currently don't know.

And we don't know how good the quality and coverage of any variables that do become available will be. So these issues will negatively affect the production of any future NZDep-like index. So we do not believe that any changes should be made to the full enumeration census until it can be confirmed that the replacement data is of adequate quality, coverage, and granularity. And for these reasons, we oppose this bill.

If the bill is passed, then we urge the committee to ensure adequate funding for Stats New Zealand and all the other government agencies who will need to be contributing admin data for the formidable data task ahead to ensure that data of adequate quality is available. And we also recommend that in the very early twenty-thirties, there's a review to implement it to investigate whether or not the data is actually suitable for New Zealand's data needs, and if not, a plan to revert to a full enumeration census.

Thank you. Any questions? Yes, HelenJust first of all, in terms for the confidence level, even with the last census, as you well know, the responses from individual households is a decreasing number. I forget exactly what it was in the last census. But to try and get back up to the confidence level, the Stats New Zealand obviously had to rely on a lot of admin data anyway. My question to you, I think you used the figure 87%, but question to you, is the increasing proportion of people who are not willing to respond to a census, are they often the people that

are the most vulnerable, or is that an unfair characterization of the type of people who are not responding to a current census form? Do you have a view on that? Look, I do not know for certain. I would certainly suspect along with you that it's the most vulnerable people who are those who possibly interact least with the census.

But the address consistency of 87%, that was you use it comparing the admin piece, APC, with the census data that existed, which had a bit of imputation going on to begin with. So that is probably, if anything, an overestimate of how good the address information is.

Okay. So in your submission, at bottom of page two, you talk about people with no access to internet at home and you say under the new approach using admin data, no and will never be able to get that information. Whereas at the moment with the census, even with a relatively low or decreasing number of households responding to censuses.

Why do you say that that is no and probably never? Because presumably what Stats New Zealand will do, they will go to all the internet providers in the country who will know every household in the country and know what the proportion wouldn't be connected. So that would be an obvious approach for Stats New Zealand to adopt.

But you just plainly say that that would never be available. I'm just asking why did you come to that view? Well, we say probably never, not certainly never. And we come to that view because it is not government data. And, for instance, Stats New Zealand's had some information available on connection to the electricity network for some time, but it is not available in the IDI, and it has not been a priority.

We don't know the future, but certainly it's unlikely to be available before there's adequate family or household data. It- Okay. Well, that's certainly a question we'll direct to Stats New Zealand. So thank you for that view on that. That's helpful. Okay. Thank you, Helen. That's been very helpful and particularly in presenting that and on behalf of Peter Crampton as well, Professor Crampton as well. So thank you both for your contributions, been very good.

Thank you. Right. Now I'm going to ask Mr. Cook to come to the front. We're obviously looking forward to your submission, Mr. Cook, given your background. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Hopefully not only because of its length. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, for the opportunity to talk.

Can I say it's my experience as government statistician in the United Kingdom and New Zealand and extensive exchanges over the years with European, Pacific, and Commonwealth statisticians that have led me to offer you the advice that you need to initiate an independent review. Is this change that simple and even necessary? Officials say that it doesn't change the legal framework governing how we collect the census.

In fact, by making the administrative data first regime mandatory for future population statistics, you are placing constraints and limits on the statistical properties of our statistics by you're actually setting in stone a path that's with untried and flexible methodology.

By deliberately removing much needed flexibility, this bill is a methodological handcuff on the independence of the statistician because you're telling them what they can't do. And I think are MPs really equipped to decide what and where statistical methods are best used for understanding New Zealand's populations?

The proposed direction shifts the system away from a direct population-wide self-reported census towards a model that relies on administrative data, data integration, and statistical modeling. This is not a minor change. It is a structural change to how population reality is constructed. A full census provides a baseline that is directly sourced from individuals, standardized across the population, and independently verifiable.

Administrative data does not provide that. It reflects who interacts with services, what those services collect, and how systems are designed. As a result, the dataset begins to reflect the system and not the population. Groups that engage less with services are recorded inconsistently or are not fully captured by existing systems, and they will be underrepresented. That's not a possibility.

They do not ask the same questions, use consistent definitions, or capture information in a way that is comparable across agencies. This leads to gaps, inconsistent classification, and reliance on estimation.

When that data is aggregated and modeled, those limitations compound. As reliance on modeling increases, outputs may appear complete, but appearance is not the same as accuracy.When data is incomplete at source and used to generate inferred results, the final data set is built on assumptions that are not always visible or testable.

This creates a condition where decisions are made on data that cannot be fully verified against reality. Alongside these risks, there is a second issue. Data is collected under specific conditions for a defined purpose, under a particular authority, and within a specific context. Under the current and proposed framework, data can be moved, combined, and reused across systems. However, there is no consistent mechanism to ensure that authority and context travel with that data, and this simply means that systems are not required to

demonstrate that the use remains consistent with the original purpose, and that authority remains valid, or that the context has been preserved. Authority is assumed rather than verified. In a system increasingly reliant on integrated and modeled data, this creates a material governance gap. Decisions are made using data where the original purpose is unclear, the authority cannot be demonstrated, and the context has been lost.

In any other regulatory environment, that would be considered a failure of control. This matters because the point of risk is no longer collection, it is use. If a system cannot demonstrate that it has authority to use data in a given context at the point it is being used, then the legitimacy of the decision cannot be assured. The census also provides an independent point of validation. It allows governments to check administrative data, identify inconsistencies, and confirm whether outputs align with the population.

If that function is reduced, there is no equivalent mechanism to replace it. Without an independent benchmark, the system becomes self-referential. These risks apply across the population, but they are amplified for Māori. The census is currently the only mechanism that allows Māori to self-identify at scale and in a consistent way.

No administrative system replaces that function. Administrative systems do not consistently capture Māori identity, iwi or hapū affiliation. And if reliance shifts to those systems, Māori are more likely to be undercounted, misclassified, or simply not captured at all. At that point, underrepresentation becomes structural, and over time, this reduces visibility.

Reduced visibility has direct consequences like funding, services, and policy decisions, because that all follows data. If Māori are not fully visible in that data, they are not fully reflected in those decisions. And there is also a contextual risk.

Māori data is connected to identity, whakapapa, and collective representation. When it is removed from its original context and combined with other data sets and reused, its meaning can change. Without mechanisms to carry forward context and authority, outputs may appear valid but not accurately reflect Māori reality.

The effects of this shift will accumulate over time. Small inaccuracies will become embedded, gaps become normalized, decisions become incomplete or decontextualized, data begin to shape outcomes. Without a reliable baseline and without the ability to verify authority and context at the point of use, that cannot be corrected.

There is also an accountability issue. The census currently provides a reference point to assess representation, track disparities, and evaluate whether commitments are being met.
